title: Auto Capture on Fulfillment
summary: Payment auto-capture on fulfillment does NOT work natively for B2B, even when location terms are set to 'due on fulfillment'. A specific API sequence can enable it, but partial fulfillments still require manual steps.
key_points: Not automatic for B2B natively — differs from DTC behavior even with 'due on fulfillment' terms
The reason: payment isn't authorized at order placement, so the buyer's revolving credit isn't tied up

Auto-capture CAN be triggered automatically if ALL of these are true: (1) order created with 'due on fulfillment' terms, (2) funds authorized via orderCreateMandatePayment, (3) order is COMPLETELY fulfilled
Partial fulfillments still require manual intervention in all cases

title: Terms & Card-on-File Requirements
summary: When a location has payment terms of any kind, a card MUST be saved to the location profile before an order can be placed. This ensures Shopify can charge the card later, outside the initial authorization window.
key_points: If the location has ANY terms (Net 30, Due on Fulfillment, etc.), a saved card is required to place an order
This applies to both storefront and admin-created orders
The requirement ensures Shopify can charge the card after the authorization window closes
Without a saved card, the order submission will fail at checkout

title: No Terms Location — Payment Behavior
summary: Locations with 'No Terms' get a 30-day card authorization, but auto-capture (full or partial) is still not possible. Custom amounts can be captured manually, and customers cannot 'Pay Now' on invoices.
key_points: Card IS authorized for ~30 days when terms are 'None'
Auto-capture (full or partial) is still not possible natively
Custom amounts can be captured up to the order total
Authorization expiry: funds can still be captured after the 30-day window — Shopify re-authorizes behind the scenes
Customers CANNOT 'Pay Now' on invoices for No Terms locations
Different cards can be used at order time without being saved to the location (since an authorization exists)

title: Deleting Saved Cards — Safe for Merchants
summary: Customers can freely delete saved cards from their location profile without risk to the merchant. The card associated with an existing order can still be charged even after deletion.
key_points: Customers can delete saved cards at any time — safe for merchants
A deleted card associated with an existing order can still be charged up to the order total
A different card on file for the same location can also be used to capture payment
Cards entered directly in Admin by a staff member can also be used to capture

title: Credit Card Authorization Windows
summary: When cards are authorized, Shopify automatically gives the longest possible authorization window (e.g., 30 days for Visa). Capturing within different windows incurs different rates — no configuration options exist. Authorizations are not always provided for B2B orders.
key_points: Authorization window is automatic — no configuration options
Capturing within standard window (e.g., 7 days for Visa) = standard fees; capturing in extended window (8–30 days) = higher rate, applied automatically
DTC On Fulfillment (No Terms): Authorization provided — yes, up to 30 days
B2B On Fulfillment (No Terms): Authorization provided — yes, up to 30 days
B2B On Fulfillment (Due on Fulfillment terms): Authorization NOT provided — order shows 'unauthorized'
B2B On Fulfillment (Due in X Days terms): Authorization NOT provided — order shows 'payment pending'

title: Variable Platform Fee (VPF) — How It's Determined
summary: VPF eligibility depends primarily on HOW payment is taken, not how the order is created. Orders where payment is brokered by Shopify (CC, ACH, Admin, POS) qualify; imported orders with external payment typically do not.
key_points: Primary factor: Is payment brokered by Shopify? (CC, ACH, Admin, POS) → VPF applies at the rate for the payment channel
B2B on POS with CC payment → charged POS VPF (not B2B VPF)
Offline payment / marked as paid / custom payment method → look at how the ORDER was brokered (storefront, admin draft, POS) → B2B VPF applies
Imported orders (created in ERP/custom app, imported to Shopify after the fact) → typically NOT VPF eligible, even if marked as paid in Admin
Exception: if payment for an imported order IS facilitated by Shopify, it becomes VPF eligible
Consult the business's VPF resources for edge cases like POS + B2B app combinations

title: Tap to Pay — B2B Limitations
summary: Tap to Pay works on iPhone and Android only — not iPad. Shopify Payments must not be in Test Mode. B2B Extend works with Tap to Pay on POS, but requires placing the order first and then collecting payment separately.
key_points: iPhone and Android only — iPad is NOT supported
Applies to both the POS app and the Shopify Admin app
Shopify Payments must NOT be in Test Mode
In the Shopify Admin app: only Draft Orders and Quick Orders are supported (not 'normal' orders)
B2B Extend works with Tap to Pay on POS
Workflow: place the order first → find it in Online Orders list → Collect Payment → select Tap to Pay

title: Charging a B2B Saved Card (or ACH) by API
summary: It IS possible to charge a B2B saved payment method (card on file or ACH bank account) via API using orderCreateMandatePayment. Any amount can be authorized — even above the order total — giving flexibility for order edits. ACH does not support auth-only.
key_points: Use orderCreateMandatePayment mutation — NOT orderCapture directly (funds aren't pre-authorized for terms orders)
Requires: Order ID, Mandate ID (from order query), and a unique Idempotency Key
autoCapture: false = authorize only; autoCapture: true = authorize and capture immediately
You can authorize an amount LARGER than the order total — useful if you anticipate order edits (e.g., shipping changes); excess triggers a refund flag
After auth-only, capture using orderCapture mutation; or void auth and re-auth using orderCreateMandatePayment
ACH bank account: cannot do auth-only — even with autoCapture: false, funds will be captured
Multiple saved payment methods on a location = multiple mandates; ACH mandate is identifiable by lack of card detail in the order query response

code_samples.label: Authorize or Capture (orderCreateMandatePayment)
Capture After Auth (orderCapture)

doc_section: Charging a B2B Saved Payment Method (Card on File) by API

title: Creating a B2B Order by API
summary: B2B orders can be created via two approaches: a two-step draft order flow (preferred when using purchasing entity fields to link Company/Location/Contact), or a single-step orderCreate mutation (preferred for ERP imports). Each has distinct payment options.
key_points: Two-step flow: draftOrderCreate → draftOrderComplete — use when you need purchasingEntity fields (Company, Location, Contact); the default card on file can be charged after completion
Single-step: orderCreate mutation — preferred for importing orders from ERP; requires an authorization token from a custom app
After single-step order creation, the order is NOT immediately payable in the customer portal — must pay via API (orderCreateMandatePayment), Admin (credit card or manual), or Send Invoice
Payment terms can be set via paymentTermsTemplateId in the draft order input

code_samples.label: Step 1 — Create Draft Order (with B2B purchasingEntity)
Step 2 — Complete Draft Order
Single-Step — orderCreate (ERP imports)

doc_section: Creating a B2B Order by API

title: Creating B2B Catalogs via API
summary: Catalogs are created in four sequential steps via GraphQL API: create Price List, create Catalog (optionally linking a Company Location), create Publication, then update the Publication to assign products.
key_points: Step 1: PriceListCreate — defines pricing rules for the catalog
Step 2: CatalogCreate — creates the catalog; can associate a Company Location directly here; capture the returned Catalog ID
Step 3: PublicationCreate — creates the publication channel
Step 4: PublicationUpdate — assigns products/collections to the publication
Full documentation with code examples available in the B2B Solutions Guide
The existing catalog creation guide is for Markets, not Company Locations specifically — logic is similar but check B2B-specific docs

title: B2B Store Credit — Admin & API
summary: B2B Store Credit (available Aug/Sep 2025+) extends DTC store credit to Company Locations. Credit lives at the location level, shared by all customers at that location. Manageable via Admin UI or API (Admin API 2025-10+).
key_points: Store credit lives at the Company Location level — all buyers at that location share the balance
Enable in Customer Account settings (single toggle for both DTC and B2B — cannot enable for one only)
In Admin: Company Location screen → 'Store credit' section → add/remove credit, set expiration date
Via API: Use Admin API 2025-10, StoreCreditAccount object; key mutations are storeCreditAccountCredit (add) and storeCreditAccountDebit (remove)
IMPORTANT: Available store credit does NOT show up in checkout when the location has payment terms; it DOES appear when paying for a past order

code_samples.label: Credit a Company Location (storeCreditAccountCredit)

title: Draft Orders — Sales Rep Has Full Product Access
summary: When a sales rep creates a manual draft order in Admin, they have access to ALL products in the store — not just what the customer's catalog allows. This is by design to give reps more flexibility.
key_points: Admin draft orders bypass catalog restrictions — reps see all products
The intended behavior: reps should have more choice than what customers can self-serve
Framing for merchants: 'We want sales reps to have full flexibility when creating manual orders'
If this is a concern, consider using the Draftable app to add structure or the Sales Rep Frontend workflow below

title: Draftable — Draft Order Helper App
summary: Draftable extends Admin draft order functionality: customers can apply discount codes, reps can add line item properties (e.g., product customizations), deduct inventory with one click, and reorder line items.
key_points: Create a checkout link from a draft order — lets the customer apply discount codes themselves
Add line item properties or order notes to draft orders (e.g., product customization details)
Multiple of the same SKU with different line item properties — useful for configurable products (e.g., custom shirts with different sizes/colors as separate line items)
Deduct inventory for all draft order line items with one click
Reorder line items within the draft order
Create a draft order from an abandoned checkout, preserving product properties

title: Sales Rep Frontend Order Submission
summary: For merchants with complex product catalogs, give sales reps a storefront-like ordering experience by creating a dedicated Company + Location for reps, with orders automatically routed to draft status for admin review and reassignment.
key_points: Setup: Create a dedicated Company for sales reps; create Location(s) for regional catalogs if needed; set location orders to go to draft; add sales reps as customers at the location
Workflow: Rep logs into storefront → browses and creates order → order submitted as draft → admin staff unlocks pricing and changes the customer to the actual buyer
This gives reps an end-user-like search experience for large catalogs without building a custom admin UI
Optional: use regional Locations to give reps access to region-specific catalogs
doc_section: Sales Rep Front End Order Submission

title: Selling Products Restricted by Shopify Payments
summary: Certain product categories (tobacco, firearms, etc.) cannot be sold via Shopify Payments. A two-store approach — one for unrestricted products (Shopify Payments) and one for restricted (third-party processor) — is the primary workaround.
key_points: Products restricted by Shopify Payments (tobacco, guns, etc.) cannot use Shopify Payments
Two-store approach: Store A = unrestricted products + Shopify Payments; Store B = restricted (and optionally unrestricted) products + third-party processor
The two stores can be partially integrated — see the B2B Field Learnings doc for integration patterns
Consider the impact on B2B catalog management, order history, and customer accounts when using two stores
doc_section: Selling Products Restricted by Shopify Payments

title: Displaying Discounted Prices on Product / Collection Pages
summary: Native Shopify discounts don't display on PDPs/collection pages because discounts are cart-aware. Three approaches exist: Compare At prices in the catalog, metafield-based calculation, or an app like Discountly.
key_points: Native discounts only surface at Cart/Checkout — not on PDPs or collection pages
Option 1 (simple): Upload Compare At prices directly in the catalog — works for straightforward discounts
Option 2 (moderate): Store discount % in metafields, calculate discounted price in Liquid/JS and render on the page — caveat: conflicts if an app is also showing a price
Option 3 (complex): Store discount ID in metafields, use app proxy to query discount amount, update prices via JS after page load
Option 4 (app): Use Discountly or similar — manages discounts outside native Shopify discounting and automatically shows discounted prices on PDPs
doc_section: Displaying Discounted prices on Product/Collection Pages

title: Custom Payment Terms (e.g., '14 Following Month')
summary: Shopify's native payment terms (Net X days, Due on Fulfillment) don't support European-style terms like '14 Following Month'. A custom app pattern using company metafields + paymentTermsSet mutation enables fully custom term logic.
key_points: Native options: Net X days, Due on Fulfillment — 'X Following Month' is NOT natively supported
Solution: store custom term configuration in a company metafield as JSON
A custom app reads the metafield at checkout and applies the computed due date via paymentTermsSet mutation
Different companies can have distinct billing cycle configurations
This requires custom development and a private/custom app

title: Shopify Tax Settings for B2B (US)
summary: US B2B tax collection is determined by three inputs: store-level nexus settings, company location tax settings, and delivery address. The combination of these three — not any single setting — controls whether tax is collected.
key_points: Three inputs determine tax: store-level nexus, company location tax setting, and delivery address
Merchant has nexus + Location set to 'Collect Tax' → tax IS collected
Merchant has NO nexus → tax is NOT collected, regardless of location setting
Location set to 'Don't Collect Tax' → tax is never collected, regardless of nexus
Location set to 'Collect Tax Unless Exemptions Apply' + state exemption on file → tax not collected; no exemption → tax collected (only if merchant has nexus)
If tax is not collected but merchant lacks nexus AND buyer lacks reseller certificate → buyer may owe Use Tax in delivery state
Always recommend merchants consult a tax professional for nexus determination

title: NetSuite ERP Integration Options
summary: Two main connectors for NetSuite + Shopify B2B: the NetSuite-owned connector (simpler, sync-direction limitations) and the Celigo connector (more flexible, bidirectional, extensible). Key limitations: neither syncs B2B catalogs or price lists.
key_points: NetSuite Connector: Product (NS→Shopify), Company (Shopify→NS as customer+sub-customer), Price & Inventory (NS→Shopify), B2B Order (Shopify→NS), Fulfillment (NS→Shopify), Refunds/Adjustments (bidirectional), Payout, Reverse Payment — ~$900/month
NetSuite Connector DOES NOT: sync B2B catalogs/price lists, export B2B order history NS→Shopify, support draft orders (only completed orders sync)
Celigo Connector: same sync types but bidirectional for products and companies; more configurable; data mapping is extensible/customizable
NEITHER connector syncs B2B catalogs or price lists — this must be managed manually or via a custom solution
Celigo is preferred for merchants needing more control over sync behavior

title: SSO / Identity Providers
summary: Shopify supports OpenID Connect for customer account SSO (external identity providers) and SAML for internal Shopify admin user SSO (including Microsoft Entra/Azure AD).
key_points: Customer accounts (B2B buyer login): OpenID Connect (OIDC) — identity provider must meet Shopify's specific requirements
Internal users (Shopify admin staff login): SAML — including Microsoft Entra (formerly Azure AD)
Review Shopify's requirements carefully before committing to a specific IdP — not all OIDC providers work out of the box

title: Site Performance & Real-Time Monitoring
summary: Shopify does not provide server log access or server-level monitoring. Shopify offers basic Core Web Vitals data natively; real-time alerting requires third-party tools like Pingdom, Datadog, or New Relic.
key_points: Shopify does NOT provide access to server logs or support server-level monitoring (e.g., CloudWatch)
Native: Shopify provides Google Core Web Vitals data (LCP, INP, etc.) — no real-time alerting
Third-party options: Pingdom (uptime/alerting), Datadog (APM), New Relic (APM)
Datadog and New Relic both integrate with Shopify — consult their documentation for setup guides

title: Inventory Checks — When They Fire
summary: For products set to NOT continue selling when out of stock, Shopify performs inventory checks at five distinct points in the buyer journey — not just at checkout submission.
key_points: Inventory checks fire at: PDP page load/refresh, Add to Cart click, Cart line item quantity update, Checkout page load/refresh, Submit Order click
Out-of-stock message is displayed to the customer at whichever step the OOS condition is detected
This prevents overselling for products configured with 'Stop selling when out of stock'

title: Themes Built for B2B
summary: The Shopify-native B2B theme is Trade. Several third-party themes also support B2B use cases, though partner validation varies.
key_points: Shopify native B2B theme: Trade
Third-party themes with B2B support: Hyper, Xtra, Yuva, Taiga
Concept theme: not built for B2B natively, but field reports indicate some merchants use it successfully
Always validate B2B-specific features (quantity rules, price display, customer accounts) with the theme developer before recommending

title: Paid Plus Trials — Cautions for Account Teams
summary: Eligible merchants can trial Shopify Plus for $399/month (1–2 months depending on region). Key risk: merchants can self-convert to full Plus without the account team, potentially causing lost deal credit.
key_points: Existing merchants globally: 2-month trial at $399 USD/month
New merchants (US/CA only): 1-month trial at $399 USD/month
Not all Plus features are available during a paid trial — check limitations before setting expectations
RISK: Merchants can self-convert to a full Plus store without account team involvement — AE must close-win the opportunity at least 3 days before trial end to preserve credit
If the store auto-converts before the CW, credit typically will not be given to the account team

title: orderCreateMandatePayment Silent Failure on Expired Cards
summary: The orderCreateMandatePayment mutation returns SUCCESS even when the card is expired — it just silently doesn't charge the card. Always verify actual payment status in the order timeline.
key_points: Known behavior: the mutation returns success (no error) even on an expired card
The card simply isn't charged — a note appears in the order timeline, but the API response looks clean
Always check the order timeline to confirm actual payment status after calling this mutation
This was discovered accidentally via an expired card on a company location
doc_section: orderCreateMandatePayment Doesn't Fail on Expired Cards

title: Re-Authorization for Extended Fulfillment Windows
summary: When fulfillment takes longer than the 7–30 day authorization window, use orderCreateMandatePayment with autoCapture: false to create a fresh pre-auth. The payment mandate outlives individual authorizations, enabling re-auth without customer involvement.
key_points: Problem: standard auth windows (7–30 days) expire before fulfillment, especially for long lead-time B2B orders
Solution: use orderCreateMandatePayment with autoCapture: false to create a new pre-auth — the mandate persists beyond any single auth
The card is vaulted against the order (separate from B2B location saved payment methods) — works even without Shopify Payments
B2B Card Vaulting (Shopify Payments only): customers can save cards on file for re-auth without intervention; IMPORTANT: only customers can vault cards — admin staff cannot do it on their behalf
Alternative: use Pre-Order apps to mark long lead-time products — automatically vaults the customer's card at purchase

title: Flow: Convert Draft Orders on Terms to Orders
summary: A Shopify Flow workflow that detects draft orders with payment terms meeting certain conditions, converts them to orders automatically, and closes the original draft. Useful for demos and as a workaround when Payment Customization functions aren't an option.
key_points: Flow detects draft orders on terms matching specified conditions
Automatically converts qualifying draft orders to completed orders
Closes the original draft order after conversion
Use case: merchants who need conditional order conversion but can't build a Payment Customization function
Also useful for demo environments to simulate B2B order workflows
doc_section: Flow to Convert Draft Orders on Terms to Orders

title: Enabling Apple Pay & PayPal for B2B
summary: Both Apple Pay and PayPal are hidden for locations with payment terms — No Terms is required for these options to show. Browser/device combination is also critical: Safari on iPhone, not Chrome on Mac.
key_points: Apple Pay: enabled once for both DTC and B2B in Shopify Payments settings
Apple Pay only shows for Company Locations with No Terms — locations with any terms hide it
Apple Pay requires Safari on a compatible device — Chrome on Mac reliably fails to show it
PayPal: same — enabled once for both DTC and B2B; requires No Terms and test mode OFF
PayPal quirk: even when PayPal Wallet is the enabled version for DTC, B2B may show PayPal Express instead
For demo environments: use iPhone with Safari for the most reliable Apple Pay demonstration
doc_section: Enabling Apple Pay and PayPal for B2B

title: Reporting on Order Custom Metafields
summary: Shopify Analytics doesn't support order.metafields, and Matrixify can't bridge the gap. Better Reports app is the recommended solution for reports combining order headers with custom metafield values.
key_points: Problem: need a report with order ID, customer name, date PLUS custom order metafields
Shopify Analytics: does not support order.metafields — no native option
Matrixify: also cannot accomplish this
Solution: Better Reports app — supports order metafields in custom report configurations
GraphQL queries can retrieve this data but provide no native export mechanism

title: Interac Payments — Canada
summary: Interac Debit works at POS via Shopify Payments in Canada (tap limit: $250/transaction). For online Interac e-Transfer, two providers exist: Windcave and PSI Payment Source. Tobacco merchants have specific compliance requirements.
key_points: Interac Debit at POS: supported within Shopify Payments in Canada
Tap limit: $250/transaction with daily tap limits — over the limit requires insert card + PIN
Interac e-Transfer for Online: two providers — Windcave (TBD status) and PSI (Payment Source)
PSI: e-Transfer only, $3,000/transaction limit; has special compliance requirements for tobacco merchants
Tobacco workaround: use 'Choose Payment Method Later' → customer contacts merchant offline → mark as paid after transfer is received

title: Fraud Control vs Shopify Protect — Status
summary: Merchants cannot use Fraud Control rules AND Shopify Protect simultaneously. Shopify Protect's status is uncertain: deprecated Feb 2025, rollback announced Jun 2025. Current guidance: don't proactively sell it, but discuss if merchants ask.
key_points: Fraud Control rules and Shopify Protect are mutually exclusive — cannot use both
Shopify Protect announced as deprecated in Feb 2025 (communicated to ENT/LA)
Termination was rolled back in June 2025 — current status is unclear
Official guidance: do not use as a selling point; you can discuss it if a merchant brings it up
Note: Shopify Protect is only for Shop Pay — not applicable to all B2B payment scenarios
doc_section: Fraud Control vs Shopify Protect Status

title: IM Digital B2B Accelerator (Tapestry)
summary: IM Digital's Tapestry accelerator offers modular B2B components (LTL quotes, inventory visibility, user management, managed quotes) starting at ~$100K. Bundled with IM Digital implementation services — not available standalone.
key_points: Modular components: LTL shipping quotes, incoming inventory dates/amounts, user-managed storefront users, managed quotes
Pick-and-choose: components can be selected individually
Pricing: starts at ~$100K, scales with implementation scope
Limitation: not offered separately — bundled exclusively with IM Digital implementation services
IM Digital team can provide deeper demos and launch support discussions

title: Mobile App Tap to Pay for B2B Trade Shows
summary: For trade show B2B sales, Tap to Pay via the Shopify Admin mobile app works on iPhone/Android but supports only Draft Orders and Quick Orders. Using a laptop at the trade show gives a fuller UI than a tablet.
key_points: Shopify Admin app Tap to Pay: iPhone and Android only (not iPad); Draft Orders and Quick Orders only — not 'normal' orders
Shopify Payments must NOT be in test mode
B2B Extend + POS: place the order first → find in Online Orders → Collect Payment → select Tap to Pay
Alternative: payment QR codes or invoice links sent to customer email for in-person engagement
Trade show tip: a laptop gives a fuller admin UI than a tablet — consider it over a POS tablet setup
doc_section: Mobile App Tap to Pay for B2B Trade Shows

title: China CDN & Shopify Performance
summary: China's Great Firewall does cause real Shopify performance issues. Shopify has a China CDN (cdn.shopifycdn.net), but domestic China selling faces major blockers (ICP license, infrastructure). Cross-border is the recommended strategy.
key_points: Confirmed: China's Great Firewall causes slow loading of Shopify script files
Shopify China CDN: cdn.shopifycdn.net provides faster delivery for China
Major blockers for China domestic selling: no ICP license (regulatory requirement) + lagging China/HK CDN infrastructure
Proxy workaround: China-facing site retrieves product details via Storefront API; order data (not customer data) sent back to Shopify via Admin API
Strategy: cross-border selling is the recommended approach — not domestic China sellers

title: LitExtension — Platform Migration Tool
summary: LitExtension is an API-based platform migration tool that transfers data between platforms automatically, avoiding Excel/CSV intermediaries. It's a one-time migration tool — not middleware for ongoing ERP sync.
key_points: API-based: transfers data between platforms via APIs — no manual spreadsheet steps
Handles data mapping automatically
Scope: one-time migration tool only — not suitable as middleware for ongoing ERP integration
Recommended by experienced SEs as a solid alternative to Matrixify for platform-to-platform migrations

title: Shop App Orders — Unexpected Tax Collection
summary: Since January 1, 2025, the Shop channel automatically collects, remits, and files taxes for ALL US orders — regardless of the merchant's nexus settings. Tax appears under 'SC Commerce Services Inc.' in reporting.
key_points: Root cause of unexpected tax: the order was placed through the Shop App/channel
Since Jan 1, 2025: Shop channel auto-collects, remits, and files taxes for all US orders
This applies regardless of the merchant's tax nexus configuration
Tax appears in reporting under 'SC Commerce Services Inc.' — not the merchant's name
Diagnostic tip: if you see unexpected tax, check the audit trail in Shop internal for tax registration changes
doc_section: Shop App Orders & Marketplace Tax (Ohio Example)

title: B2B Features in Partner Development Stores
summary: Partner dev stores support all B2B API operations and end-to-end flows, but cannot test real payment collection, live third-party integrations, or Shopify Payments. Flip to a CW store when Shopify Payments testing is needed.
key_points: CAN test: complete B2B GraphQL API, end-to-end flows (company → checkout), catalog and pricing logic, customer account experience, payment terms setup, integration points
CANNOT test: real payment collection (test transactions only), production payment terms enforcement, live third-party integrations, real-world performance/load
Limitation: cannot connect Shopify Payments in test mode on a partner dev store
When you need Shopify Payments: flip to a CW (Converted to Paid) store
doc_section: B2B Features in Partner Development Stores

title: Reserving Inventory for B2B vs DTC (Separate Contexts)
summary: Separating inventory reservations between B2B and DTC contexts is a known merchant need. See the source doc for full implementation details from the team.
key_points: Merchants often need to ring-fence inventory for B2B buyers separately from DTC stock
See the source Google Doc for full implementation patterns from this team discussion
doc_section: Reserving Inventory for B2B vs DTC (Separate Contexts)

title: POS Customer View App (Android)
summary: An official Shopify app turns an Android device into a customer-facing display at POS — showing the cart and order total to the buyer while the cashier uses the main POS device.
key_points: Turns an Android device into a customer-facing POS display
Available on Google Play Store
Official Shopify app — see Help Center for setup documentation
Useful for B2B merchants with a physical counter or showroom environment

title: Restricting Products to B2B (or DTC) Only
summary: Native B2B functionality (catalogs, companies, Markets) can gate products to specific buyer segments — showing certain products only to B2B customers or only to DTC customers, including handling unassigned account holders.
key_points: Use Markets + native B2B (catalogs, companies) to limit product visibility by buyer type
Can restrict certain products to B2B customers only, or conversely show products only to DTC customers
Also handles customers who sign up for an account but aren't assigned to a company — they fall into the DTC/unassigned context
Video resources available in the source doc for both use cases

title: Creating a New Demo Store (Staff Store)
summary: Staff demo stores are created via Forge, upgraded to Plus through Services Internal, and exempted from app charges via an Experiments flag. No credit card required for apps once the flag is set.
key_points: Step 1: Create a Staff store via Forge
Step 2: Upgrade to Plus via Services Internal
Step 3: In Services Internal, find your store and click the 'Avoid app charges' button
Step 4: Copy your Shop ID from Services Internal, go to the Experiments page, add an assignment with your Shop ID
Step 5: Install apps — they should show a message confirming no charges; no CC required
If apps ask for a CC, uninstall and reinstall after the Experiments flag is set

title: Flow: Flag Fraudulent Orders on Address Mismatch
summary: A Shopify Flow workflow that flags potentially fraudulent orders when the shipping and billing addresses don't match — augmenting Shopify's built-in fraud score with a custom rule.
key_points: Shopify provides an automated fraud score by default based on millions of transactions
This Flow adds a custom rule: flags orders where shipping ≠ billing address
Useful for merchants who want to augment native fraud detection with their own logic
Flow can be extended with additional merchant-specific rules
doc_section: Fraud Detection Flow for Address Mismatch

title: Flow: Tag Orders with Staff Email for Sales Attribution
summary: A Flow workflow that tags orders with the assigned staff member's email address, enabling sales attribution reporting — since Shopify has no native way to attribute orders to specific reps.
key_points: Problem: no native way to report on sales attribution in Shopify
Solution: use Flow to tag orders with the staff member's email address at order creation
Can be modified to tag with name or any other staff property instead of email
Tagged orders can then be filtered in Shopify Analytics or exported for reporting
doc_section: Tag Order With Assigned Staff Emails

title: POS International Markets Limitation & Hybrid Architecture
summary: POS does not support international markets — each country with POS needs a separate Shopify admin. The recommended pattern: consolidate B2B into one admin using Markets, keep separate POS stores per country until platform support arrives.
key_points: POS does not support international markets functionality — confirmed by Jeff St. Pierre (POS Expert)
Each country requiring POS must maintain a separate Shopify admin
Retail Markets allows location-based pricing (online vs. in-store) but not international currency/tax handling for POS
Recommended hybrid architecture: consolidate all B2B into one admin (using Markets), maintain separate POS stores per country
B2B and POS can operate independently — no requirement to mix them in one admin
Monitor Shopify roadmap for international POS markets support

title: Delivery Date Picker for Company Locations
summary: No off-the-shelf app supports Company Location-aware delivery date scheduling. Checkout Blocks can't access Company data directly. A custom dev workaround exists: pass company metafields to checkout as checkout metafields before checkout begins.
key_points: Use case: B2B alcohol merchant where each company location has specific agreed-upon delivery days (e.g., Company A = Tuesdays only)
Checkout Blocks limitation: cannot directly access Company or Company Location data — only customer data is available
Workaround: pass company metafield data to a checkout metafield via cart page customization before checkout; use checkout.metafields in Checkout Blocks to read it
Use customer.b2b variable for B2B detection to conditionally show/hide the date picker
No Flow triggers exist for cart/checkout creation — can't automate the metafield passing natively
No off-the-shelf app supports this — requires custom development with Checkout Extensibility experience
doc_section: Delivery Date Picker for Company Locations

title: Web Application Approaches — SPA vs SSR vs SSG vs MPA
summary: A reference for SEs to understand the four main web rendering approaches when evaluating merchant tech stacks or headless implementations: SPA, SSR, SSG, and MPA — each with different trade-offs for commerce.
key_points: SPA (Single Page App): one HTML page, JS handles all routing; best for web apps/dashboards/admin panels — examples: React + Vite
SSR (Server-Side Rendering): HTML built on server per request, good for SEO, dynamic data; best for e-commerce — examples: Next.js, Remix
SSG (Static Site Generation): pages pre-built at build time, extremely fast, requires rebuild for updates; best for blogs/docs/marketing — examples: Gatsby, Astro
MPA (Multi-Page Application): traditional, each route is a separate HTML page, full page refresh; best for content-heavy sites — examples: WordPress, Rails
For headless commerce evaluation: most Shopify headless implementations use SSR (Next.js/Remix) for SEO + dynamic pricing; SPA for admin/dashboard tools

title: High Variant Count & Image Limits
summary: Even with the 2048 variant limit, products still cap at 250 images — so you can't have one image per variant at scale. Storing images in variant metafields bypasses this limit entirely.
key_points: Variant limit: 2048 per product (new limit); Combined Listings limit: 2000 variants
Image limit: 250 images per product — does NOT scale with the variant limit
At scale, you cannot have one image per variant with the 250 image cap
Workaround: store variant images in metafields on the variant — these do NOT count toward the 250 image limit
Traditional fallbacks still apply: split into multiple products, or use a configurator app

title: Using Flow + Sidekick (AI-Assisted Flow Building)
summary: Sidekick can create Shopify Flows for merchants (or help them build flows). This is a useful capability to demonstrate and recommend for merchants who want automation but lack technical resources.
key_points: Sidekick can generate Shopify Flow workflows from natural language descriptions
Useful for merchants who want automation but don't have developers
Refer merchants to the Sidekick Flow FAQ doc for supported capabilities and examples
Good demo moment: show how a merchant can describe a workflow in plain English and get a Flow built

title: AI-Generated HTML Decks (vs. Google Slides)
summary: Build polished, browser-native presentation decks using Cursor + Claude instead of Google Slides. AI writes all HTML/CSS/JS from your outline. Host on Quick Sites internally or deliver as a standalone .html file to clients — no Google account required.
key_points: Use Cursor + Claude: prompt with your deck outline (topic, sections, tone), Claude generates a full self-contained HTML presentation
Presenter features (added by Brandon Anderson): speaker notes with timer, slide selector grid, laser pointer, '?' key to show all keyboard shortcuts
Internal hosting: deploy the file to a Quick Site for shareable internal access (https://slide-deck-exploration.quick.shopify.io/)
Client delivery: share as a standalone .html file attachment — opens in any browser, no Google account or Slides access needed
Version-controlled: treat the HTML file like code — commit to a repo or Quick Site for history and iteration
Faster iteration than Slides: re-prompt Claude to change design, layout, or content in seconds; no drag-and-drop formatting
Terry Kealey and others on the B2B SE team have adopted this workflow for customer-facing decks

code_samples.label: Presenter keyboard shortcuts
code_samples.code:
← / →   Previous / next slide
F       Fullscreen
L       Toggle laser pointer
G       Open slide selector grid
S       Toggle speaker notes
?       Show all keyboard shortcuts

title: Pipe17 — ERP Integration Option
summary: Pipe17 is a strong third-party ERP connector option when full bidirectional sync and support for all object types (including draft orders and catalogs) is required. Recommended when long-term B2B growth is the goal.
key_points: Supports all sync flows — including draft orders and B2B catalogs/price lists (unlike native NetSuite connector)
Bidirectional sync across all major object types
Pricing: ~$24,000/year for up to 4,000 orders/month
Best fit: merchants investing heavily in B2B who need all objects (customers, catalogs, orders, draft orders) synced automatically
Experienced team with strong support reputation among SEs
